Author: Julius Herfel
Title: Problem in the Loop: The model risk in making machines think
Text:
Problem in the Loop: The model risk in making machines think
Why do we actually need models? Albert Einstein was able to derive Relativity simply with ‘thought experiments’. The mathematical proof came later. This is where the definition of a model is important. A model is not a model because it is in a spreadsheet or made of numbers. A model maps inputs to outputs via assumptions. Just like a brain. But neither can replace the other (yet). Problem in the Loop is the twelfth and penultimate article in Layered Beliefs.
A quick executive summary for busy professionals
- Decisions emerge from accumulating evidence across dimensions that no single parameter can capture, whether in consumers’ minds or in trading algorithms.
- AI aims to mimic the brain’s logic by applying reinforcement learning or drift-diffusion logic. However, it substitutes correlation for causality, a practical workaround that bakes structural model risk into every output, from hallucinations to agentic amplification loops.
- Model risk in AI is not a governance problem or cybersecurity challenge, but rather an inherent limitation of approximating human reasoning with statistics, making complementary human oversight over the model’s internal logic itself non-negotiable.
After nearly 200,000 testers had completed a blind study, arguably the largest in consumer products history, Coca-Cola introduced New Coke in 1985. A year before its 100th anniversary, the fizzy drink producer felt it was time for a new recipe. Sweeter, smoother, lighter, less acidic: the test subjects agreed — overwhelmingly, consistently, statistically significant and repeatable. New Coke was poised to be a massive success.
79 days after New Coke had hit the supermarket shelves, the original was back, tens of millions of dollars worth of New Coke concentrate were written off. What had happened?
Once the blind conditions were removed, the public decided that taste alone wasn’t enough to change a favourite drink. Coca-Cola did not consider that history, culture, the feeling of youth and freedom were all part of the Coca-Cola experience. New Coke did not provide any of that. When the classic Coke came back, it was more successful than ever before.
The Mind as Model Engine
The end of New Coke also meant the end of a brief period of intense euphoria at Pepsi, which knew Coca-Cola’s dilemma too well. Pepsi would also regularly beat the traditional Cola in blind studies, yet it never became as popular. But why?
The blind studies were comprehensive and the results obvious, yet the nearly flawless model was overridden by the consumers’ mental model. A mental model is like an internal version of a financial model — it takes in information, applies assumptions, and outputs conviction.
Whereas Coca-Cola measured the expected success of New Coke with a single parameter — relative taste — the mental model of the consumers considered various additional factors.
The lesson is not that Coca-Cola should have asked more questions. The lesson is that human decisions emerge from accumulating evidence across dimensions. No single-parameter model can capture this. This is where drift-diffusion becomes essential.
Drift-Diffusion Models
Models often replicate brain functions — including those of animals. If you have ever raised and trained a puppy, you will know that parks are full of temptations even for well-trained dogs.
Dog owners will be familiar with this scenario: Your dog spots another dog about the same distance from her as you. You want the dog to come back, but within the dog there is a visible inner conflict that probably goes like this:
Left: I heard ‘come’, that means I must go left.
Right: But the other dog looks interesting.
Left: My owner will give me a treat if I go to him.
Right: But maybe the other dog has something better than treats.
Left: I see a treat in his hand, so I will get one if I go back.
…you get the idea. It is fascinating to watch this inner conflict until from one second to the next, the dog runs off or comes back to you.
This process is known in statistics and behavioural science as a drift-diffusion model, and those models are present in most intelligent animals — humans included. It describes how decisions form not in an instant, but through the gradual accumulation of evidence for competing options. Each new signal — the smell of another dog, the tone of your voice, the sight of a treat — nudges the mental “pointer” a little to one side.
Traders work in much the same way. Every price tick, news headline, or forum post adds a small piece of evidence to their internal scale. When enough signals align, conviction crosses a threshold, and they act.
Algorithmic-trading engines, radar target-tracking, and high-frequency order-execution models all apply drift-diffusion logic. The machine does not “think” any more than the dog does — it follows a quantitative version of the same principle: evidence drifts toward a boundary until one option becomes inevitable.
Modern LLM decoding strategies like beam search or nucleus sampling echo this principle at a surface level: candidate tokens are evaluated against cumulative probability distributions until a selection threshold is reached.
Where Brains and Models Are Alike
Brains and models both simplify on purpose, and both define the key components of a system and the cause-and-effect relationships between them. Both are used to answer “what if” questions. But the how can differ significantly.
This ‘inner scale’ of the dog, for example, is more than just a calculation; it is the birthplace of what we might call ‘meaning.’ In behavioural science, a concept known as Variable Ratio Reinforcement describes why dogs learn better when they don’t get a treat every time they do something right. When the reward is not guaranteed, the brain doubles down. It begins to look for a deeper pattern, a story, or a ‘belief’ to justify the effort.
For the dog, the owner stops being a mere vending machine and becomes a partner. For the human trader, the market stops being a random number generator and becomes a narrative of ‘value’ or ‘opportunity.’ Meaning is effectively the narrative the brain constructs to explain why it persists in the face of uncertainty.
When we train a model, we use mathematical versions of these rewards by adjusting gradients and minimising error. But because a model lacks the biological ‘why’, it can mimic the persistence of the puppy, but it cannot feel the weight of the belief that makes that persistence meaningful.
There are many decision-making models. Drift-diffusion is among the leading frameworks dominating the current neuroscience discourse. The others are predictive coding for the perception of reality, model-based learning for predicting and “what if” analyses, and reinforcement learning for value formation. Some of those terms you might have heard in a different context: AI.
Why We Still Need (Non-Brain) Models
Our brain has not evolved for all computing operations. Mental models are intuitive, qualitative, fuzzy, malleable and personal — they cannot be objectively tested. Therefore, we need mathematical models as a complement: falsifiable, rigid, precise, quantitative and formal.
How AI Approximates Causality
Science fiction often explores what it means to be human through characters or societies that represent our polar opposite. Chief among these is Star Trek, and especially the character Data. The android was built to be a perfect logical engine, yet he wants nothing more than to be human.
Crucially, his creator Dr Soong didn’t build a ‘tin man’; he built a machine that looks exactly like a person — Dr Soong. This choice might be a quirk of the eccentric scientist, a decision by the writers, or simply an attempt by the producers to give actor Brent Spiner more screen time.
However, it says something profound about our current approach to AI: we aren’t just trying to build a powerful tool; we are trying to build a mirror with superhuman abilities.
This complementarity explains why we build AI to do exactly what we do: write text, brainstorm, make restaurant bookings, sort good and bad apples in a factory.
But there are key differences: reinforcement learning in humans (and puppies) rewards with dopamine pulses; an AI doesn’t feel “good”, but an adjustment of gradients is triggered. Model-based learning in humans works on causality, in Monte Carlo simulations on correlations.
Here is the crux: to ‘mimic’ the rewiring of reward circuits, we force the AI to reshuffle its internal mathematics. To make a model as well as an AI make causal decisions, we use correlations — this is a reasonable choice as we build these models not to have to code every cause and effect relationship. At the same time, every statistics primer will firmly emphasise not to confuse correlations with causality.
Whenever I put a leash on my dog for her morning walk, my neighbour sings. An AI would infer that me leashing my dog prompts my neighbour to sing. In reality, 5:30 am is the time I take my dog to play fetch. It is also the time my neighbour, who happens to sing under the shower, gets up and showers.
AI gets better at reasoning, but its entire internal logic remains correlation-based. A “hallucination” in an LLM happens because it has a high-conviction toward a word, but lacks the model-based world map to realise that the word is factually impossible.
“You can know the name of a bird in all the languages of the world, but when you’re finished, you’ll know absolutely nothing whatever about the bird… So let’s look at the bird and see what it’s doing — that’s what counts.” — Richard Feynman, What Is Science? 1966
AI Model Risk
To build an AI that speaks or reasons like humans, but to approximate causality through correlation is a practical workaround. But it is a model choice, just like using a copula for default modelling. All the training data in the world cannot override this choice. It remains a breaking point in AI.
Whether it is the design and specification, the implementation choices, or how we use AI, human choices to approximate brain functions and human biases when defining inputs, methodology and test parameters inevitably lead to model risk. Hence, before worrying about cybersecurity and governance, we need to worry about model risk.
In The Big Mistake, I have dissected two attempts to get out of AI model risk: the narrative that agentic AI reduces model risk to a non-material factor, and the idea that “model” can be redefined as something without human intervention.
The agentic AI claim is a catastrophic misunderstanding of model risk and a ticking bomb. Not only do the loops in agentic AI amplify model risk, agents also introduce operational risk. Where in classical models model risk substitutes operational risk, here both reinforce one another.
Redefining what a model is does not change the risk of it. Even language models won’t be impressed by a linguistic switcheroo and will continue to hallucinate.
The correlation-for-causality substitution means AI shares the same fundamental vulnerability as any financial model: human design choices create structural risk.
AI model risk needs to evolve to handle the explainability, cascade and content-dependency problems that emerge from this design, but this remains a model risk challenge at its core, not a cyber risk problem, and not something solved by a governance dashboard. Firing the modeller will not solve model risk.
The Limits of AI
The ambition of many tech enthusiasts is of course to create an AI that is unbound from human imperfection and biases, just like Data in Star Trek. This is the intellectual crux: physically you can make an AI look like Brent Spiner or any other person, conceptually, replicating the perfect AI Data represents is still light years away.
Attempts to create a fully independent AI or a completely value-free model cannot escape human choices as input. For a long time, trading algorithms were considered “neutral” as all they do is use minuscule market inefficiencies for arbitrage in split seconds — data based, value ignorant. It works most of the time, but there are still two very human ideas behind it: the efficient market theory, postulating markets will always return to an equilibrium, and monetary incentives.
If we found a way for an AI to create itself without human inputs, it is unlikely it would be a value-free, neutral trading algorithm because the trading algorithm still has a purpose: make money. As neutral and value-free as it is, it still requires the desire to buy things with money — and that is a human input, not a neutral law of mathematics.
Wrap-Up
I have in the previous articles often said that ‘belief replaces facts’ and ‘our financial system is built on layered beliefs’. This sounds negative — because we often use human irrationality to praise technology and mathematical models — but I do not see it as negative.
Relying on ‘either/or’ is a problem. Relying exclusively on subjective beliefs while discounting mathematical evidence is equally perilous; it fosters an environment where misinformation and conspiracy theories can flourish unchecked.
I am optimistic that over decades AI will move closer to the ideal model which can reason like a human, yet ignore our shortcomings. Until then, brains and models need to go hand-in-hand, two complementary calculators solving problems together each using their strength.
